Step 2: 	The Vital Connection
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X

Jon: 	Okay, let’s get into the tutorial now for Step 2 of 5 of the 5-step VSL
process, and it’s called The Vital Connection. And here, our goal is to
begin building that deep rapport with your viewer. That is the primary
essence of The Vital Connection.
The good news is, if you’ve done your homework, if you’ve done your
assignments, you’re already 90% done with this section. That’s cool,
isn’t it? 90% done. It’s going to go fast, because we’re going to be
using your nightmare story for most of this section.
Now, if you haven’t written your nightmare story yet, either pause the
video and write it now, or keep listening to me and write it at the end of
this tutorial, because you’re going to need it. Okay? You’re going to
need your nightmare story for this. It’s the ideal place to put it.
Now, here is The Vital Connection formula. Ready? It’s starts with
modest introduction, your nightmare story, a transition into the big
problem, and that’s step 3 of the 5-step video sales letter process,
about the system (and I’ll explain what that means), and results from
the system. And it ends with the final transition phrase.
So, modest introduction. We’re already covered this, and I’m going to
cover it again. nightmare story. Transition into the big problem, into
step 3. About the system, results from the system, and the final
transition phrase, just one phrase.
Let’s dig in.
The modest introduction. This is listing your credentials and your
expertise in a modest way, by using the “I wasn’t always” technique.

-- 1 of 12 --

2
We’ve covered this before. In fact, I’ve covered it in the video in, I
believe, the nightmare story. So, the nightmare story video, if you’ll
watch that video again, you will find the “I wasn’t always” technique
covered. But we’re going to cover it again here, just to make sure that
you get it down.
“I wasn’t always a best-selling fitness author, and I certainly wasn’t
always a guy you’d see on the cover of a fitness magazine. Heaven
forbid.” So, that is an example of a modest introduction.
You would say the same thing. I’ve listed credentials here. Let’s say I
have a PhD in physics and now I know how to create this certain
product that allows you to sleep better at night. And I’m completely
making this up. “I wasn’t always a PhD in physics and I certainly
wasn’t always a prize-winning inventor. In fact,” and this is where you
go into the “in fact” part, and this is setting your user up for what? For
your nightmare story.
See, “I wasn’t always an expert at bed and breakfasts. I wasn’t always
having an award-winning bed and breakfast. In fact, I got started in
this whole thing through a nightmare experience through a bed and
breakfast. I was just like you.” Again, we’re building the “just like you”
rapport. Okay?
So, that is the modest introduction. That’s all there is to it. You just
want to say, “I wasn’t always like this. In fact, I was completely
different.”
And let me give you some tips here. I don’t want you to use pictures of
yourself yet. Okay? Even if that picture seems really tempting.
Now, for a fitness guy like me, a picture right off the bat, of me flexing
my stomach or abs or muscles or whatever, not a good idea. I have
not built rapport. It’s not time, yet. I want to still build a little bit more
rapport.
Is that a make or break? It can be, it can be. I’ve seen video sales
letters work that have pictures right off the bat, but the ones that work
the best do not. So, I prefer that you leave those pictures for later.
And when you come out of this phase of The Vital Connection, and
then you show the picture, wow. It will have a lot of meaning.
But until they see what you’ve gone through and hear what you’ve
gone through, the picture isn’t going to mean nearly as much. Let me
give you an example of what I’m talking about here.
Let’s say that I came up to you and you’re a single guy. And I said,
“Hey, I’ve got this girl. She is really cute and she wants to meet you.

-- 2 of 12 --

3
Here’s her picture.” And I showed you a picture. And let’s say, in your
eye, she’s a 7 or a 6, average girl. And you’re going, “Okay, well, I’m
not really that interested.
But if I told you, “Hey, I got this girl I want you to meet, and she
escaped from a prison in Russia, and came over here and educated
herself, became a PhD in physics.” We’ll go back to the physics
analogy. “And now she’s an instructor at MIT. Here’s her picture.” All
of a sudden, you’re like, “Wow, this is awesome,” especially if that guy
that I’m talking about happens to be interested in physics.
All I’ve done is build rapport. I’ve built rapport based on something. I
didn’t just show him the picture off the bat. All of a sudden, that girl
looks beautiful. And so it’s a much more higher interest to meet that
person.
That is the same exact technique that you’re going to follow by not
showing pictures of you right off the bat or pictures of your product,
pictures of your service, pictures of the results, whatever. Not yet.
Okay?
2nd tip. What I do want you to use is social proof pictures. For
example, here’s one thing I can do. In my example below, “I wasn’t
always the best-selling fitness author that you see right below here,”
under the red example. I could choose to include a magazine cover,
because I mention a fitness magazine, as long as I’m not on the cover.
I don’t want them to see a picture of me yet. Okay? They haven’t
heard my horror story. They don’t know how far I’ve had to come to
get to where I am.
So, what I would do instead and have done, is to say, “I wasn’t always
a best-selling fitness author, and I certainly wasn’t a guy you’d read
about in all these popular magazines.” And underneath that, you
would see these magazines pop up. And it would just be their logos,
like Flex, Shape, Muscle, and Fitness, etc, etc.
What I’ve done here is dropped social proof. Social proof is a lot
different than pictures. And since you can’t use testimonials at this
point of view – somebody asked me where they should put their
testimonials before – and a lot of times I get that question. And I say,
“Wait on the testimonials.”
This is a testimonial you can put right at the start, because you’re not
saying anything specific. If you happen to have been on NBC news, if
you happen to have been on a magazine, or you happen to be
featured in something that people would know, you can use that at this
point, just as a graphic.

-- 3 of 12 --

4
Now, this leads into your nightmare story. Simply insert your entire
nightmare story into your VSL, at this point. And I want you to do so by
using the one sentence technique. If you don’t remember what that is,
please go back and look at the 3X VSL Toolbox video.
The one sentence technique is simple, though. You probably don’t
need to do that. Because it’s just one sentence per slide followed by
an ellipses. That’s it, ….
Now, that is optional, but I have found that an ellipses really help move
people forward, because when you see a ..., what do you think? The
first thing you think? “Oh, there’s more.” That’s what you want your
reader to be thinking. There’s more. There’s something more to
follow. He’s not quite finished with that sentence yet.” Even if it
doesn’t make any sense grammatically, I use it all the time.
And then, every 5 to 7 slides, I want you to use the paraphrase
technique. Guess where that’s found? In the 3X VSL Toolbox video.
Now you know why we gave you that video, alright?
This is simple. You sum up your sentence every 5 to 7 slides, using
only 3 to 5 words. And you do that so that you break the user’s
pattern.
Right now, they’re seeing every word you’re saying on the screen, and
then all of a sudden, on the 5th or 6th slide, they see “really killer
technique” or something like that. And you’re going, “You know, and
I’m also going to cover a really awesome technique for building your
abs, and it requires no sit-ups at all.” And all it says is, “Really killer
technique,” or “Awesome abs,” 2 words. “Awesome abs.”
That’s what’s really powerful about this technique is you can basically
paraphrase what you’re saying, and your viewer does not know when
you’re going to end. So this reconnects them back into the slides.
Otherwise, you’re breaking the hypnosis effect, right here. You are.
Because it is very hypnotic, reading every single word. But when you
break it, it’s a pattern break. And then when you continue it, you’re just
engaging them more and more and more.
This one technique, I’ve seen increase conversions by 50% on video
sales letters. 50%! I’ve actually created a video sales letter that did
very well, and the entire thing was paraphrased. In other words, I
didn’t have every word I was ever saying on the screen, until, I think,
the very end. And I used it into my pitch.
So, use the paraphrase technique. You’ve already got this written, if
you’ve done your assignments. You have the template, so put your

-- 4 of 12 --

5
entire nightmare story into the video sales letter. Break it up into one
sentence at a time, so that they’re only seeing one sentence on a slide.
Remember, we want the slides to go by fairly rapidly, because that
creates a hypnotic effect. Follow the sentence by the ellipses.
And then every 5 to 7 slides, just pick. It doesn’t have to be specifically
5 to 7, but just about 5 to 7, pick the slide, paraphrase it down to 2 to 5
words, 3 to 5 words, so that you hold your reader’s interest.
Now, examples of these techniques are going to be found within the
PowerPoint template in the member’s area, so please download that
template. Or, if you’re a Mac user, you can use the Keynote template.
Now, this takes us to the next step, which is the transition to the big
problem. The big problem is step 3, that we’re going to cover in the
next video; step 3. So, this is the transition.
You want to begin your transition into step 3 simply by reminding your
viewer that you’re just like them, with one crucial difference: you have
a system. And you can feel free to use any word you want there. You
have a system, you have a formula, you have a plan, you have
strategy, you have a blueprint. There’s one of a dozen words you can
use here.
And that’s what the only difference between you and the person
watching the videos. So, “I’ve been through hell, I’ve come out of it.
Now, you may think I’m this famous fill-in-the-blank person, or I’m this
guy that knows all about making money on the internet. Yes, that’s
true, but the only real difference between you and me is I figured out a
system.”
Remember back in the very first few videos, where we talked about
becoming a reluctant expert or an expert by necessity? If you don’t
remember that, watch the videos. You have become an expert by
necessity. That’s the whole point of the nightmare story, other than
rapport, is to set yourself up as an expert through necessity.
“Because I went through this really hellish bed and breakfast
experience” – I’m just using a bed and breakfast as an analogy here –
but “I went through this hellish bed and breakfast experience; I became
an expert on the subject. I became an expert because I just decided I
never wanted to go through this again. And I wanted to make a place
that my family could go to that I would enjoy, and all my friends would
enjoy. So, I didn’t set out to be an expert, but I became one. Now, I’m
kind of world-famous.”
“And the only difference between me and you is I went through all this.
And I’ve come up with a plan to build a strategy for the ideal bed and

-- 5 of 12 --

6
breakfast,” or “a strategy for the ideal water skiing unit,” or “a strategy
for the ideal,” whatever your product is.
“I came up with a blueprint.” I love the word blueprint. One of my
books actually has the word blueprint in the title, I like it so much. So,
blueprint is awesome.
Formula, like 3X VSL Formula. There you go. Practice what we
preach.
Here’s an example. “Remember.” See how I’m tying every tip and
every video together?
“Remember, despite my success today, I’m no different than you. I’m
just a guy who happened to stumble upon a system.”
Now, I love this stumble, because I didn’t really set out to build a
system to lose weight. I kind of stumbled on it. It’s like, okay, I just got
tired of doing this and I kind of found it by accident. And there’s a huge
degree of truth to that. I had no idea about this whole eat up, eat
down, eat up, eat down thing, this caloric staggering thing. I just kind
of stumbled on it, and it became a system, became a formula for
eating.
So, if you have a story like that, this is an ideal place to say this. I start
by saying, “Remember.” I draw the user right back in. “Despite my
success today,” and you can make that even more elaborate. You can
say, “Despite the fact today that I’m on magazine covers and people
think of me as ‘fitness guru,’ I’m really no different than you. I’m just a
guy who happened to use what I went through and came up with a
really cool system. I’m a guy with a system.”
And that takes us right to the system part. Again, change that word to
formula, blueprint, plan, whatever you like. We’re going to call the
system as the steps, though.
And here’s where I want you to insert your image, okay? But not an
image of your product. I want you to put an image that represents the
success or benefits that your product delivers to your reader, to your
viewer.
So, in my case, this may be a picture of me because that is kind of the
picture of the success that people can have. I’ll show my before
picture, where I look like this completely obese slob with a mullet. No
kidding. And I show my after picture. It’s a pretty stunning difference.
So now, they’re kind of connected. They’ve heard the hell that I went
through, the fact that I stole donuts, the fact that I was depressed to

-- 6 of 12 --

7
the point of suicide. I’ve written multiple nightmare stories. And the
other case, they’ve heard the fact that I thought I had a heart attack in
118 feet of water, because I’m a scuba diver, and talk about a scary
story. Wow.
So, at this point, they connect with me. They’re in rapport. So, when
they see the picture of me before and after, they’re like, “Hey, you go,
guy.” That’s pretty awesome. That’s pretty awesome.
If I showed them right off the bat, that picture, they’re like, “Yeah, what
an arrogant jerk. Oh, yeah, he probably thinks he’s full of himself.” But
now they know that I’m not.
Do you see the importance, the absolute importance of the vital
connection, of getting into that nightmare story, of telling your story?
And even if you don’t have a truly hellish nightmare story, just telling
about your experience that led you up to this will still build that rapport.
And that’s the key.
Let’s say I didn’t want to use a product of myself. I may want to use a
product of a very fit woman, a smiling woman holding an apple. This is
where I want to bring in an image. It breaks the pattern, but it also sets
them up to understand the system. And that connects the system to
that result.
When you show an image in a specific way, at a specific time, you
connect what you’re saying – in this case the system – to the result,
the image that they’re seeing.
Just be cautious of one thing. And that’s that you don’t want to
assume a specific image, unless it’s yourself or unless it’s your service.
Now, you don’t want to give away the product yet, okay? So it might
have something to do with your service. But if you use a specific
image like, for example, if I used a picture of a guy that was a Mr.
Olympia bodybuilder with the veins all over his abs and 3% body fat,
how many people would be turned off by that? A lot. A lot. Despite
the fact that you can use my system and look like that if you want to. I
don’t, and most normal people don’t want to either. But I want to look
a smaller version of that, a guy that doesn’t do drugs, a guy that want
to ruin his life. I want to look like a healthy version of that.
But, most people don’t even want that. They just want to just lose
some weight. Right? So, I want to give them an image that’s not over
the top, that gets the broad spectrum feeling of what my product can
do for people, okay?

-- 7 of 12 --

8
Now, on this slide, this is very important. You’re going to want to use
an inclusion statement. 3X VSL Toolbox. Might as well just write that
down. “Re-watch 3X VSL Toolbox video.” Kind of important, under the
1-2-3 Formula.
An inclusion statement to further connect you to your viewer. You’re
going to build an even deeper rapport with this one simple statement.
Here’s an example. This is an image that I was talking about earlier
that I might want to include here. And I’ll say, “This is a system (or
alternate term) that allows people like us to experience blank.”
Notice the huge difference here between what I’m saying, who I am,
the picture that I’m showing, and the inclusion statement. I’m a guy
obviously, and what I’m talking about; weight loss. And I’m talking
about my hellish experience; I just got out of that. I tell them that I
found a system. I show them maybe my picture, or look at this. I show
them a picture of a woman on a scale, happy. I am not a woman on a
scale, happy. That’s a very general picture. And notice that she is not
like super-scrawny. She’s just in good shape.
So this is a very generic, in shape, happy woman picture. But they’re
hearing a man’s voice. So, what does that tell them, psychologically?
The system works for men and women.
So, if you have a product or a service that’s not gender specific, you
might want to think about this. It’s a good idea.
But look what else I’ve done. “It allows people like us.” And I get kind
of folksy. I’ll say, “Folks like us.” And I’m not a folksy speaker, I’m not
a folksy person, but I like the generality of that. “It allows people like
you and I,” if you want to get grammatically correct, you and me, or
whatever. But, “That allows people like us, normal people like us to
experience.”
And what does that mean? What I’ve done is I’ve just connected
myself even deeper into rapport. “People like us.” I didn’t say
specifics. “People like us, who happen to be 65 pounds overweight
and who…” No. Because, again, I don’t want to insult the reader.
They may be 300 pounds overweight. They may be 10 pounds
overweight. It’s just “people like us,” because we’re all in this thing
together.
And once you get in something together, once you have that kind of
rapport, when you get to the end, when you get to the grand offer and
you say, “Look, I’m giving this to you. We’ve gone through all this thing
together, we’ve been through so much together, and this is all I’m
requesting, to give you all of this value,” then you’ve built a huge, huge

-- 8 of 12 --

9
reason for someone to say yes. You’ve built rapport.
Then, you go into a little bit more about the system. This is followed by
3 results of your product, each of which are positive in nature.
Remember, we’ve just come out of a really hellish, negative thing in
the nightmare story. So now, we’re going to go positive. We’re going
to turn the values around.
So, here’s what I’m going to say. “This is a system that allows people
like you and me, and people like us, normal people,” you can say
anything you want there, as long as it’s inclusion, “to live a life free
from the burdens of endless weight gain, to enjoy the freedom of
eating foods we really love and enjoy and still lose weight, and
experiencing the excitement of seeing the body of our dreams in the
bathroom mirror every morning.”
See, I just picked 3 positive results. Notice these aren’t specific
benefits. I didn’t say, “It allows people like us to eat our favorite foods
specifically by lowering calories and raising calories.” No, I’m not
getting into specifics. These are very general. And guess where those
3 were taken from? They were taken from – go ahead, say it – my
USP. My USP.
These are the 3 benefits of my USP. “Wow, Jon, now I see why we
wrote the USP first, because it continually comes into play throughout
the entire VSL process.”
So, I just took my 3 USP’s and just went plop.
Now, I expanded on the last one, “experiencing the excitement of
seeing the body of our dreams in the bathroom mirror.” That played off
of one of my USP benefits, but you get the idea.
So I give 3 positive results in nature. So the entire thing again is, “This
is a system that allows people like us to experience living life free from
the burdens of,” and so on and so forth; 3 positive examples, each of
those which are vague in nature, and they’re big, huge benefits of the
system.
Okay, notice the wordsmithing in the sentence above. A lot of
wordsmithing. And in case you don’t know where wordsmithing comes
from, 3X VSL Toolbox. Just tattoo that on your arm or something, like
Josh tattoos everything on his arm. A friend of mine, Josh.
Okay. So you want to notice the wordsmithing. Look at the freedom,
the freedom. I talk about freedom all the time. “Free from the burdens
of endless weight gain. Enjoy the freedom,” okay? Look at the word
enjoy, used over and over again.

-- 9 of 12 --

1
0
Experiencing. 	This is one of the most powerful things in marketing.
People do not buy something from me because they want to get
something. They want to buy something from me because they want
to experience something. And I don’t care if it’s the most boring
product on earth, I don’t care if it’s the most exciting product on earth,
it’s all about experience, and the more you can use the word
“experience,” and tie that into what they will experience.
For example, if you have a make money online product, you don’t want
to say, “You can experience making money online.” That’s not what
they want to do. That’s not what they’re wanting to experience.
What they’re wanting to experience is the freedom that comes with
making money online. So, you can say, “You’re going to experience
the freedom of getting up and going wherever you want, whenever you
want, and never having to worry about what, where, and pay your
bills.”
That’s the experience. That’s what people want. Your system
product/service/whatever is merely the means to help get them the
experience they seek.
I use the word “excitement,” a power word. And I use one other thing.
I used an anchor. An anchor is something that everyone knows. Just
think of it that way. So I said, “Now you get to see the body of your
dreams in your bathroom mirror.” That’s an anchor, because I’ve
anchored the body of your dreams to something everyone has, which
is a bathroom mirror.
Okay, that’s about they system. We move on to the results of the
system.
Personalize, then share the experience with a result that you’ve
experienced using the system. What do I mean by that?
Here’s the example. “This is the same system that has allowed me to
effortlessly drop 78 pounds of blubber.” Okay? That’s my experience
with my own system. That’s all I’ve done. I’ve shared with my own
system.
“And keep it off for more than a decade.” That’s pretty cool, but it goes
further. “And” – where did I get the and word? Yeah, that last video.
“And it’s the system I’ll be sharing with you today, so you can finally
see the results you desire, too.”
How many times did I say the word “you”? 3 times in one sentence.
Okay? Remember, it’s always “you,” “your.” It’s not “me,” “mine,” “I.”
Stay away from those words, except here. This is when you

-- 10 of 12 --

1
1
personalize the experience. You personalize what you went through,
the results that you got from your own system. “Now I can experience
fill-in-the-blank.” And this is the same system that will teach you and
show you how to experience the same things, too.”
So, you’ve personalized, then you’ve reversed it back to them. See
how that works? That’s the results of the system.
And then, it ends with the final transition phrase. This is a direct lead-
in to the big problem that also opens another loop. So now, we have
what’s called a nested loop. Okay? Don’t worry about the technical
terminology here. We’ve opened 2 loops, and we haven’t closed any
of them yet.
We opened the loop at the very first, if you’ll remember, and said, “I’m
going to give you all this killer information, but first I’m going to tell you
a story.” Open loop. We haven’t closed it yet.
Now, we’re opening a second loop. It’s nested. Okay? So, in other
words, there’s 2 open loops going on at the same time. This really,
really draws people into the equation, because they’re expecting you to
finish the first one and now you’ve got the second one open.
So, this is how you do it. A couple of examples. “And, I’ll share all the
specifics of that system with you in just a moment. First, I must tell you
about the real problem that we face.”
What am I doing here? I’m transitioning into my big problem part,
which is step 3. We’ll get to that in the next video session. But we’re
just transitioning into what the big problem is. There’s conspiracy
going on. There’s some problems coming up you need to know about
before I tell you about the specifics of the system, and before I tell you
about all those cool tips I told you about.”
So, you’ve got 2 loops going on here. “So I’m going to share the
specifics of that system with you, just a moment.” So, in other words,
we want to keep them forward, keep people pushing forward. But
“First,” and you don’t use the word but. Just say, “First, I must tell you
about the real problem.”
“This is the hidden foe” – notice how I’ve highlighted that in red –
“behind why you are gaining weight in the first place. And most people
have no idea that it’s not their fault. You will find there’s a magical
phrase, a magical phrase called ‘It’s not your fault.’” Write those 4
words down: ‘it’s not your fault.’“
Now, I’ve gone over this in another video. I make that more ethically
sound by saying, later in the video, “This is not your fault. You’ve been

-- 11 of 12 --

1
2
misled by a lot of false information. It’s now your responsibility to take
action,” which is the truth. “A lot of people in my particular industry,
they’ve gained weight, and they don’t realize that they’re falling into
traps that have been set for them by the media, that have been set for
them by magazines, that depend on them spending money over and
over and over again, the supplements that promise bogus weight loss
claims.”
Every industry has this. Every industry has this. You can go to any
service, any industry, has got a hidden foe. We’re going to talk about
finding that in the next video session.
But for now, this is the transition. I’ve just opened another loop and I’m
transitioning into the next step, step 3, which is the big problem.

-- 12 of 12 --
